EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were noted across over half of Northern Craven County from January thirteenth through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed along the I-40 corridor in the North Carolina foothills and Piedmont during the afternoon of the 24th, beginning as a mix of snow and sleet. The precip quickly transitioned to mostly sleet, which continued into the overnight and early morning of the 25th. The sleet began to mix with freezing rain after sunrise on the 25th, briefly becoming all sleet before ending early in the evening. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with up to 3 inches reported across Davie County. Ice accretion was Generally around 0.1 inch, with amounts up to 0.25 inch reported closer to the Blue Ridge escarpment.

An arctic air mass overspread the southern Appalachians in the wake of a strong cold front throughout the 26th and 27th. Widespread air temperatures in the single digits combined with gusty northwest winds to produce wind chill values of 5 to 10 below in the valleys. Temperatures near or below zero combined with generally stronger winds to yield wind chills of 15 to 25 below above 4000 feet.

Severe Drought conditions were introduced across the North Carolina and Virginia Piedmonts in early January after the previous two months of November and December recorded precipitation totals of 2.00-4.00 inches, which ended up being 25 to 50 percent of normal for that period. These dry conditions combined with the dry fall conditions leading up to below average rainfall in November and December, contributed to a rapid decrease in groundwater and soil moisture in this region, where observed values for each had fallen to at or below the 10th percentile for groundwater, and 2nd to 5th percentile for soil moisture in the first 0-100 cm based off the NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated on the 6th of January. Streamflows similarly decreased across the area. This included the Yadkin River, which was observed to be below the 10th percentile per the USGS WaterWatch website and, in some cases, were near record-low flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were introduced to Rockingham county in early January after the previous two months of November and December recorded precipitation totals of 2.00-4.00 inches, which ended up being 25 to 50 percent of normal for that period. These dry conditions combined with the dry fall conditions leading up to below average rainfall in November and December,  contributed to a rapid decrease in groundwater and soil moisture in this region, where observed values for each had fallen to at or below the 5th percentile for groundwater, and the 2nd to 5th percentile for soil moisture in the first 0-100 cm based off the NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated on the 6th of January. Streamflows similarly decreased across the area. This included the Dan River, which was observed to be below the 5th percentile per the USGS WaterWatch website and, in some cases, were near record-low flows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 5 to 10 inches across the county. Car crash temporarily shut down I-95S north of Lumberton with no injuries reported. School and government offices closed day after the storm. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 7 to 10 inches across the zone, with an isolated area of 10 to 18 inches in the western part of the zone near the state line. County schools were closed the two days following the events. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 5 to 10 inches across the zone. Official snow total at the NWS Wilmington airport was 5.8 inches. MLK Parkway, Isabel Holmes Bridge, and Cape Fear Memorial Bridge were all closed during the snowfall. County and city offices and facilities were closed the following day, with schools moving to remote learning. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 7 to 9 inches across the zone. Impacts from the storm were limited.

Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 24th, beginning as a mix of sleet and snow. The precipitation quickly transitioned to all sleet before mixing with freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 25th. Mostly freezing rain fell during the daylight hours before the wintry precip ended late in the afternoon. Total sleet and snow accumulations generally ranged from 0.5-1.0 inch, with around 0.25 inch falling on top of that. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in several days of travel difficulties.

Arctic high pressure arrived by January 27 in the wake of a departing winter storm. However, the pressure gradient between the high arriving and the low departing provided gusty winds that combined with the cold air to provide dangerously low wind chills. Wind chills dropped below -15��F during the late evening hours of January 26 into the early morning hours of January 27 in Ashe and Watauga counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet weather stations reported wind chill values dropping to -15��F or lower. The lowest wind chill value recorded was -28��F at Mount Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed in areas along and near the Blue Ridge escarpment during the late evening of the 24th, beginning mainly as sleet. The sleet, occasionally mixed with snow continued into the early morning of the 25th, when it began to mix with and eventually change to freezing rain. The freezing rain was mostly light until a band of heavy freezing rain moved over the area late in the afternoon. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with around 0.5 inch (locally as high as 0.75 inch) ice accretion on top of the sleet, resulting in very hazardous driving conditions. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in travel difficulties that lingered for several days.

An area of low pressure developed across Texas and moved eastward towards the Appalachian Mountains, while Arctic high pressure also tracked eastward across the northern Plains and Great Lakes. The Arctic high reached the Appalachian Mountains first and wedged southward against the eastern slopes of the Blue Ridge to provide cold air near the surface. Meanwhile, the area of low pressure dissipated across the mountains, while a secondary area of low pressure formed off the North Carolina coast. This pattern set up a Miller B winter storm with the precipitation type as initially snow on January 24 but changing over to sleet and freezing rain by January 25 due to a substantial warm nose aloft. Notable impacts were documented during this winter storm in the form of power outages and transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix occurred with reports of up to 3.5 inches of snow, up to 2.0 inches of sleet, and up to 0.05 inches of freezing rain. Key Impacts: power outages, transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the North Carolina mountains as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the afternoon of the 30th and continued off and on for more than 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the morning of the 31st. By the time the snow tapered off during the late afternoon, widespread 6-10 inch totals were reported, with 12-18 inches occurring across the high elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across Texas and moved eastward towards the Appalachian Mountains, while Arctic high pressure also tracked eastward across the northern Plains and Great Lakes. The Arctic high reached the Appalachian Mountains first and wedged southward against the eastern slopes of the Blue Ridge to provide cold air near the surface. Meanwhile, the area of low pressure dissipated across the mountains, while a secondary area of low pressure formed off the North Carolina coast. This pattern set up a Miller B winter storm with the precipitation type as initially snow on January 24 but changing over to sleet and freezing rain by January 25 due to a substantial warm nose aloft. Notable impacts were documented during this winter storm in the form of power outages and transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix occurred with reports of up to 3.1 inches of snow, up to 2.6 inches of sleet, and up to 0.05 inches of freezing rain. Key Impacts: power outages, transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the North Carolina mountains as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the afternoon of the 30th and continued off and on for more than 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the morning of the 31st. By the time the snow tapered off during the late afternoon, widespread 6-10 inch totals were reported, with 12-18 inches occurring across the high elevations.

A southern stream disturbance brought moisture over a very cold low level airmass on January 25 and 26th, 2026. Precipitation began as snow late on January 24 across the region but transitioned to freezing rain as temperatures warmed aloft on the 25th and 26th of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured 0.25 of freezing rain near Ahoskie, NC.

A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 6 to 10 inches across the zone, with isolated area of over 10 inches around Ocean Isle Beach. Schools were closed the two days following the storm. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed along the I-40 corridor in the North Carolina foothills and Piedmont during the afternoon of the 24th, beginning as a mix of snow and sleet. The precip quickly transitioned to mostly sleet, which continued into the overnight and early morning of the 25th. The sleet began to mix with freezing rain after sunrise on the 25th, briefly becoming all sleet before ending early in the evening. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with up to 3 inches reported across Davie County. Ice accretion was Generally around 0.1 inch, with amounts up to 0.25 inch reported closer to the Blue Ridge escarpment.

An arctic air mass overspread the southern Appalachians in the wake of a strong cold front throughout the 26th and 27th. Widespread air temperatures in the single digits combined with gusty northwest winds to produce wind chill values of 5 to 10 below in the valleys. Temperatures near or below zero combined with generally stronger winds to yield wind chills of 15 to 25 below above 4000 feet.

Severe Drought conditions were introduced across the North Carolina and Virginia Piedmonts in early January after the previous two months of November and December recorded precipitation totals of 2.00-4.00 inches, which ended up being 25 to 50 percent of normal for that period. These dry conditions combined with the dry fall conditions leading up to below average rainfall in November and December, contributed to a rapid decrease in groundwater and soil moisture in this region, where observed values for each had fallen to at or below the 10th percentile for groundwater, and 2nd to 5th percentile for soil moisture in the first 0-100 cm based off the NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated on the 6th of January. Streamflows similarly decreased across the area. This included the Yadkin River, which was observed to be below the 10th percentile per the USGS WaterWatch website and, in some cases, were near record-low flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were introduced to Caswell county in early January after the previous two months of November and December recorded precipitation totals of 2.00-4.00 inches, which ended up being 25 to 50 percent of normal for that period. These dry conditions combined with the dry fall conditions leading up to below average rainfall in November and December,  contributed to a rapid decrease in groundwater and soil moisture in this region, where observed values for each had fallen to at or below the 5th percentile for groundwater, and 5th percentile for soil moisture in the first 0-100 cm based off the NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated on the 6th of January. Streamflows similarly decreased across the area. This included the Dan River, which was observed to be below the 5th percentile per the USGS WaterWatch website and, in some cases, were near record-low flows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific and Gulf moisture arriving from the southwest rode up and over shallow arctic air at the surface across the eastern Carolinas on January 25, 2026, leading to a thin glaze of freezing rain and some sleet.  The largest accumulations of freezing rain, up to 0.35 in thickness, was reported from Lumberton and Red Springs in Robeson County, NC, as well as Florence SC.  Lesser amounts of ice were reported from most other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KLBT ASOS measured 0.33 of ice. Over 1000 homes was without power Sunday evening across Robeson county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 30 and ended on February 1, ranging from 5 to 9 inches across the county. Government offices closed day after the storm with remote learning at county schools. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved eastward along the Gulf Coast on January 30 and intensified as it reached the Georgia and South Carolina coastline by January 31. This single low pressure system combined with Arctic air to the north to produce a Miller A winter storm where all precipitation fell as snow due to the absence of any warm nose aloft. Snow totals ranged from about six inches to a foot across northwest North Carolina. Impacts from this storm were mainly transportation delays as power outages were limited due to the snow being dry and fluffy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 4.0 to 7.5 inches. Key Impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure along the Georgia coast on 31 January intensified as it tracks off the North Carolina coast into the early morning hours of 1 February. With abundant cold air in place with cold high pressure over the northern Middle Atlantic, widespread snow fell across central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 13 to 16 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low tracked south and then southeast from the Upper Midwest to the Southeast during the weekend of Friday January 30th to Sunday February 1st. At the same time, a baroclinic zone set up along the offshore waters of Georgia, westwards to the Deep South. A surface low then developed along this baroclinic zone just off the coast of Georgia during the early morning hours on Saturday. As the upper level low neared the developing surface low, this resulted in a Miller-A low pressure system. The associated offshore low pressure system then rapidly deepened as it tracked northeastwards just off the North Carolina coast during the overnight hours between Saturday and Sunday. This very strong low then pulled away from the Carolinas and moved out into the open waters of the North Atlantic during the late morning hours on Sunday. With an unusually cold airmass in place across the Southeast and Mid-Atlantic, a rather widespread and historic snowstorm for almost all of Eastern North Carolina occurred. Snow began during the morning hours on Saturday mainly along the Crystal Coast and Inner Banks, though a period of light snow occurred across portions of the Coastal Plain as well Saturday morning. By Saturday afternoon the low pressure system off the coast began to deepen quickly resulting in heavier and more widespread snowfall across much of Eastern North Carolina. Snowfall rates became heavy at times resulting in periods of whiteout conditions as 30-50 mph wind gusts (60-65 mph on the Outer Banks) also impacted the area making travel treacherous across the region. Snow finally pushed completely offshore by the late morning hours on Sunday. Over 10 inches of snowfall were measured across most counties of Eastern North Carolina outside of Hatteras Island and Ocracoke with several counties reporting over a foot of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread reports between public, CoCoRahs, trained spotters, and NWS employees of ten to nineteen inches of snowfall including fifteen inches of snowfall at the NWS Newport Morehead City office. Snowfall occurred between the morning of January thirty-first and the morning of February first.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 24th, beginning as a mix of sleet and snow. Increasing southwest winds overnight resulted in steadily warming temperatures allowing the precipitation to change to freezing rain and rain across the Smokies and vicinity by daybreak. By mid-morning, precip had changed to rain across the entire area. Total sleet and snow accumulations generally ranged from one tenth to around one half inch. Some high elevation locations as well as some locations in the upper Tuckasegee and upper Pigeon valleys also saw a few hundredths of an inch of ice accretion.

Snow finally pushed completely offshore by the late morning hours on Sunday. Over 10 inches of snowfall were measured across most counties of Eastern North Carolina outside of Hatteras Island and Ocracoke with several counties reporting over a foot of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported snowfall totals of sixteen inches near Hubert, North Carolina, with several additional reports from the public, and CoCoRaHS stations reporting between twelve and seventeen inches of snowfall across Onslow County. Snowfall fell between the morning hours on January thirty-first and the morning of February first.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the mountains and foothills of North Carolina as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the late evening of the 30th and continued off and on for around 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the morning of the 31st. By the time the snow tapered off, 5 to 8 inch totals along with locally higher amounts were reported across the area.

Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 24th, beginning as a mix of sleet and snow. Increasing southwest winds overnight resulted in steadily warming temperatures allowing the precipitation to change to freezing rain and rain across the Smokies and vicinity by daybreak. By mid-morning, precip had changed to rain across the entire area. Total sleet and snow accumulations generally ranged from one tenth to around one half inch. Some high elevation locations as well as some locations in the upper Tuckasegee and upper Pigeon valleys also saw a few hundredths of an inch of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm developed on 24 January and did not fully let up until early on 26 January. After a strong Arctic cold front arrived the prior day, an area of low pressure tracked across the southeastern United States, bringing a plume of moisture into the Carolinas. A wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow developed, with the greatest snow and sleet accumulations over the Piedmont and the highest ice accumulations over the Sandhills and Coastal Plain of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow and sleet fell across the county, with also up to a tenth of an inch of freezing rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half inch to an inch of snow and sleet fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low tracked south and then southeast from the Upper Midwest to the Southeast during the weekend of Friday January 30th to Sunday February 1st. At the same time, a baroclinic zone set up along the offshore waters of Georgia, westwards to the Deep South. A surface low then developed along this baroclinic zone just off the coast of Georgia during the early morning hours on Saturday. As the upper level low neared the developing surface low, this resulted in a Miller-A low pressure system. The associated offshore low pressure system then rapidly deepened as it tracked northeastwards just off the North Carolina coast during the overnight hours between Saturday and Sunday. This very strong low then pulled away from the Carolinas and moved out into the open waters of the North Atlantic during the late morning hours on Sunday. With an unusually cold airmass in place across the Southeast and Mid-Atlantic, a rather widespread and historic snowstorm for almost all of Eastern North Carolina occurred. Snow began during the morning hours on Saturday mainly along the Crystal Coast and Inner Banks, though a period of light snow occurred across portions of the Coastal Plain as well Saturday morning. By Saturday afternoon the low pressure system off the coast began to deepen quickly resulting in heavier and more widespread snowfall across much of Eastern North Carolina. Snowfall rates became heavy at times resulting in periods of whiteout conditions as 30-50 mph wind gusts (60-65 mph on the Outer Banks) also impacted the area making travel treacherous across the region. Snow finally pushed completely offshore by the late morning hours on Sunday. Over 10 inches of snowfall were measured across most counties of Eastern North Carolina outside of Hatteras Island and Ocracoke with several counties reporting over a foot of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall reports from the public showed between thirteen and seventeen inches of snowfall fell across Jones County between midday of January thirty-first and the morning of February first.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm developed on 24 January and did not fully let up until early on 26 January. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between one and 2 inches of snow and sleet fell across the county, with isolated spots of two and a half inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed along the I-40 corridor in the North Carolina foothills and Piedmont during the afternoon of the 24th, beginning as a mix of snow and sleet. The precip quickly transitioned to mostly sleet, which continued into the overnight and early morning of the 25th. The sleet began to mix with freezing rain after sunrise on the 25th, briefly becoming all sleet before ending early in the evening. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with up to 3 inches reported across Davie County. Ice accretion was Generally around 0.1 inch, with amounts up to 0.25 inch reported closer to the Blue Ridge escarpment.

Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 24th, beginning as a mix of sleet and snow. The precipitation quickly transitioned to all sleet before mixing with freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 25th. Mostly freezing rain fell during the daylight hours before the wintry precip ended late in the afternoon. Total sleet and snow accumulations generally ranged from 0.5-1.0 inch, with around 0.25 inch falling on top of that. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in several days of travel difficulties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the southern North Carolina Piedmont during the early morning of the 25th. Snowfall rates became heavy at times resulting in periods of whiteout conditions as 30-50 mph wind gusts (60-65 mph on the Outer Banks) also impacted the area making travel treacherous across the region. Snow finally pushed completely offshore by the late morning hours on Sunday. Over 10 inches of snowfall were measured across most counties of Eastern North Carolina outside of Hatteras Island and Ocracoke with several counties reporting over a foot of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall reports from the public, trained spotters, and Fire Departments showed between eleven and seventeen inches of snowfall fell across Beaufort County between the morning of January thirty-first and the morning of February first.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm developed on 24 January and did not fully let up until early on 26 January. After a strong Arctic cold front arrived the prior day, an area of low pressure tracked across the southeastern United States, bringing a plume of moisture into the Carolinas. A wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow developed, with the greatest snow and sleet accumulations over the Piedmont and the highest ice accumulations over the Sandhills and Coastal Plain of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around an inch of snow and sleet fell, along with trace amounts of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low tracked south and then southeast from the Upper Midwest to the Southeast during the weekend of Friday January 30th to Sunday February 1st. At the same time, a baroclinic zone set up along the offshore waters of Georgia, westwards to the Deep South. A surface low then developed along this baroclinic zone just off the coast of Georgia during the early morning hours on Saturday. As the upper level low neared the developing surface low, this resulted in a Miller-A low pressure system. The associated offshore low pressure system then rapidly deepened as it tracked northeastwards just off the North Carolina coast during the overnight hours between Saturday and Sunday. This very strong low then pulled away from the Carolinas and moved out into the open waters of the North Atlantic during the late morning hours on Sunday. With an unusually cold airmass in place across the Southeast and Mid-Atlantic, a rather widespread and historic snowstorm for almost all of Eastern North Carolina occurred. Snow began during the morning hours on Saturday mainly along the Crystal Coast and Inner Banks, though a period of light snow occurred across portions of the Coastal Plain as well Saturday morning. By Saturday afternoon the low pressure system off the coast began to deepen quickly resulting in heavier and more widespread snowfall across much of Eastern North Carolina. Snowfall rates became heavy at times resulting in periods of whiteout conditions as 30-50 mph wind gusts (60-65 mph on the Outer Banks) also impacted the area making travel treacherous across the region. Snow finally pushed completely offshore by the late morning hours on Sunday. Over 10 inches of snowfall were measured across most counties of Eastern North Carolina outside of Hatteras Island and Ocracoke with several counties reporting over a foot of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall reports from trained spotters, the public, and CoCoRaHs data showed between ten and twelve inches of snowfall fell across mainland Hyde County between the morning of January thirty-first and the morning of February first.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the southern North Carolina foothills around midnight early on the 25th. The precipitation began as mostly sleet, which continued through the early morning hours before mixing with, and eventually changing to freezing rain later in the morning. The precipitation finally tapered off during the early evening. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with around 0.25 inch of ice accreting on top of the sleet. This made for very hazardous driving conditions. Due to unusually cold weather following the event, several days of refreezing resulted in travel difficulties continuing during the overnight and morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy snowstorm struck portions of southwestern North Carolina at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow led to heavy snow accumulations: 5.7 inches on the east-southeastern side of Murphy, along with 4 inches elsewhere in Murphy. Additional snow totals include 3.8 inches of snow near Hot House, and 3.4 inches of snow near Nottely Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy snowstorm struck portions of southwestern North Carolina at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snowfall was observed northeast of Hayesville, along with a report of 2 inches of snowfall southwest of Hayesville near the Georgia state border.
